title: Modi in Melbourne: Wide-ranging Australia-India Cooperation Deepens
gist: Modi's visit to Australia, featuring a 35,000-person Docklands Stadium event, showcased India's diaspora soft power and the BJP's donation-cultivation strategy, while Australian politicians increasingly court Indian Australians, now the country's largest and electorally significant foreign-born group.

insights.claim: Modi's visit to Australia was more festival than diplomatic exchange, including a Melbourne Docklands Stadium event that attracted 35,000 people alongside meetings with PM Anthony Albanese and business leaders.
insights.speaker: Grant Wyeth
insights.so_what: Illustrates how India projects influence via diaspora spectacle, a growing feature of its Indo-Pacific statecraft.
insights.locator: As has become the norm in countries

insights.claim: Large diaspora events serve as India's soft-power projection and a way for Modi's BJP to cultivate links with and attract donations from diaspora communities.
insights.speaker: Grant Wyeth
insights.so_what: The BJP's diaspora fundraising and mobilization model has cross-border political and financial dimensions worth tracking.
insights.locator: Such events are part of India's soft power projection

insights.claim: Indian Australians have rapidly become Australia's largest foreign-born group and are now an electorally significant force that Australian politicians increasingly court.
insights.speaker: Grant Wyeth
insights.so_what: The demographic weight of Indian Australians deepens the structural pull toward closer Australia-India ties.
insights.locator: But these events are also increasingly seen
